data.title: Wheat mixed, corn up 1-3 cents, soy down 3-5
data.description:
The following are U.S. expectations for the resumption of grain and soy complex trading at the Chicago Board of Trade at 8:30 a.m. CDT (1330 GMT) on Friday.

WHEAT - Down 1 to up 1 cent per bushel

  • Wheat futures traded near flat, pressured by abundant global harvests and rain in wheat growing regions.

  • Traders adjusted positions at the end of the month and before a long weekend in the United States, where financial markets will be closed on Monday for the Labor Day holiday.

  • Abundant rainfall and strong soil moisture levels are expected to benefit Argentina's 2025/26 wheat crop, which is forecast to post above-average yields, the Buenos Aires Grain Exchange said on Thursday.

  • Rains in the U.S. Plains and France are expected to benefit the crop in the coming days, according to forecaster Vaisala.

  • CBOT December soft red winter wheat (WZ25) was last down 3/4 cent to $5.28-1/4 per bushel. K.C. December hard red winter wheat (KWZ25) was last down 1 cent to $5.14-3/4 per bushel, and Minneapolis December wheat (MWEZ25) was last down 1/4 cent to $5.77 a bushel.

CORN - Up 1 to 3 cents per bushel

  • CBOT December corn (CZ25) ticked up, buoyed by strong weekly export data.

  • The USDA on Thursday reported weekly export sales of corn at 2,071,900 metric tons.

  • U.S. farmers are on track to harvest the nation's biggest corn crop in history this autumn.

  • CBOT December corn (CU25) was last up 2 cents to $4.12 per bushel.

SOYBEANS - Down 3 to 5 cents per bushel

  • Soybean futures edged lower as a lack of Chinese demand for U.S. supplies hung over the oilseed market.

  • Soybean prices had climbed to a two-month peak last week, buoyed by hopes that China would revert to buying U.S. crop after months of shunning the origin in a wider trade war with Washington, but no such purchases have been confirmed.

  • China's soybean importers are boosting purchases from Argentina and Uruguay, adding to flows from main supplier Brazil, according to trade sources

  • Traders will be monitoring U.S.-Chinese talks in the coming days, with senior Chinese trade negotiator Li Chenggang expected in Washington.

  • The USDA reported weekly export sales of soybeans at 1,183,400 metric tons.

  • CBOT November soybeans (SX25) were last down 3-1/4 cents at $10.44-3/4 per bushel.

data.title: Dura Publishes Q2 2025 Interim Report
data.description:
Dura reports revenues of 179.5 MSEK (164.8) and EBITDA of 41.3 MSEK (34.4) for the second quarter of 2025. On a pro forma basis for the last twelve months, the Group reports revenue of 563 MSEK (460) and EBITDA of 55 MSEK (49).

Statement from Group CEO

“We are pleased to report a strong second quarter with year-on-year growth in both revenue and EBITDA. We continue to successfully execute our strategy to build a market-leading group within sustainable real estate maintenance. An important part of this strategy is selective acquisitions, which we have delivered on through CC Plåt & Tak, acquired in May, and Gårdsmark, completed after the end of the quarter. With CC Plåt & Tak, we strengthen our presence in the important Stockholm market, while Gårdsmark broadens our service offering by adding drainage solutions.”

— Niclas Winberg, Group CEO

Statement from Group CFO

“The Group reports 22% revenue growth and a 12% increase in EBITDA for the last twelve months on a pro forma basis. With the addition of CC Plåt & Tak and Gårdsmark to the Group, we continue to expand both our service offering and our national presence. Investments made in strengthening our sales organization earlier in the year have already generated significant returns”

— Alexander Singer, Group CFO

The full Q2 2025 interim report is attached to this release.

data.title: Paradigm Gold Announces Non-Brokered Private Placement
data.description:
Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - August 29, 2025) - Paradigm Gold Corporation ("Paradigm" or the "Company") reported today that it plans to issue up to 9,000,000 units in a non-brokered private placement for total gross proceeds of $1,050,000.

The Company will issue up to 6,000,000 flow-through units ("FT Units") at a price of $0.125 per FT Unit to raise $750,000, and issue up to 3,000,000 non-flow-through units ("NFT Units") at a price of $0.10 per NFT Unit to raise $300,000 (collectively, the "Offering"). Each FT Unit will consist of one flow-through common share and one-half share purchase warrant (each whole warrant, a "Warrant"). Each NFT Unit will consist of one non-flow-through common share and one-half Warrant. Each Warrant in the Offering will be exercisable to purchase one non-flow-through common share at an exercise price of $0.15 for a period of three years from the date of issue. Finders' fees and commissions may be paid by the Company in relation to the Offering. The securities issued under this Offering will be subject to a hold period of four months and one day from issuance. The Offering and payment of finder's fees is subject to TSX Venture Exchange acceptance.

Net proceeds from the Offering will be used for working capital and to fund costs associated with an exploration program on the 100% owned Swift-Katie, gold/copper project in the "Golden Arc" district in British Columbia, consisting of five priority targets identified over a 2,500m strike-length.

Swift-Katie Cu-Au Project, BC. 100% owned

The Swift-Katie project is located 7 kilometers southwest of Salmo, British Columbia, Canada, has excellent access via road and infrastructure, and benefits from a year-round exploration advantage. The large land package consists of 19 MTO and Legacy mineral claims totaling approximately 8,797 hectares, which comprises two claim groups underlying the Katie copper-gold-silver porphyry deposit (a possible analogue to the producing Copper Mountain Mine at Princeton, BC) and the Swift gold target (possible analogues to gold-veins in the nearby Rossland Mining District) (+2.7Mozs Au production).

2025 Exploration will primarily focus on the Swift Gold target, where previous drilling by the company in 2017 returned multiple high-grade gold intercepts extending along an approximate 1.75km strike-length of the target structure which remains largely untested. Highlights from 2017 drilling include 2.5m of 11.5g/t Au and 6.7g/t Ag in hole SK17-015; and 8.6m of 3.1g/t Au and 2.0g/t Ag in hole SK17-019.

Historical results from trenching in another area of the Swift Target include 2m averaging 100.1g/t Au, outlining the potential for areas of increased gold grades along the structure. The reader is cautioned that these results are historical in nature, have been provided for information purposes only and have not been independently verified by the company.

Drilling to date on the Katie Target has identified three areas of significant Cu-Au mineralization, defined in 72 historic drill holes, which when modelled, form a semi-continuous shell of anomalous copper-gold mineralization over an 1800m cumulative strike. Highlight assays from the company's drilling on the Katie Target include 101.5m of 0.19% Cu and 0.12g/t Au from VKT08-069 and 64m of 0.16% Cu and 0.29g/t Au from VKT08-071. The Katie Target is being permitted as a secondary drill target.

data.title: Archer-Daniels-Midland to cease operations at Bushnell plant
data.description:
Grain trader Archer-Daniels-Midland said on Friday it plans to cease operations at its plant in Bushnell, Illinois, as part of its plan to streamline its soy protein production network.

The company said its recently recommissioned flagship facility in Decatur, Illinois, is expected to improve operating profit over time.

ADM has been cutting jobs and downsizing certain operations since announcing in February that it planned to reduce costs by $500 million to $700 million over three to five years.

The company said in April it would permanently close a South Carolina soybean processing plant to cut costs.

data.title: Sidara: This Means Bidco Doesn't Need Consent of the Panel to Invoke Any of the Exceptional Conditions
Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0923 ET - Marvell Technology's 2Q results and 3Q guide are slightly weaker than J.P. Morgan analysts expected, as strong artificial intelligence-spending trends didn't translate into a material upside for the semiconductor company. Green shoots in the business are being offset by some lumpiness across end markets, they say in a research note. However, the analysts believe the company's AI-related programs and products are strong enough to ultimately drive growth this year, and into next year. "Overall, we see a solid setup for the company, driven by the continued recovery in its cyclical businesses and sustained AI growth tailwinds," the analysts write. J.P. Morgan lowers its price target by $10, to $120. Shares fall 14% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Now that Remy Cointreau faces 15% tariffs from the U.S., instead of 30%, the French liquor company's decision to reinvest the difference is a key positive, Jefferies analysts write in a note. Remy reduced its estimates of the U.S. tariff hit on fiscal 2026 operating profit to 20 million euros, down from 35 million euros. As the cognac industry recovers in the medium term, the maker of Louis XIII cognac will too, analysts say. Though timing on its rebound is uncertain, the decision to focus on strategic growth in China and the U.S. is a bright spot, they add. Shares fall 1.5% to 53.85 euros. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0916 ET - Dell's gross margin miss will fuel concern from investors about the company's ability to grow sales while boosting margins, but there are signs that the company's margins should pick up from here, say Melius analysts. The company will soon be able to attach higher-margin services and storage to its data servers and margins will grow as "higher margin enterprise versions of AI servers become a bigger piece of the pie," the analysts say. J.P. Morgan analysts also note that the company is confident that supply chain inefficiencies that cut into Dell's 2Q margins have been fixed. Shares fall 6.9% to $124.79. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0904 ET - Gold futures trade sideways, and are on track to end the week higher on interest rate cut expectations and safe-haven demand. Futures are flat on $3,475.30 a troy ounce, and are up 1.7% on week. The precious metal is trading at the upper end of its recent range of price. This reflects Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's signals about a U.S. rate cut, unprecedented political pressure from the White House on the Fed and persistently high levels of geopolitical tension, FxPro's Alex Kuptsikevich says in a note. President Trump's introduction of 50% tariffs against India risks deepening international divisions, as well as encourages the process of de-dollarization and diversification of central banks' foreign holdings, Kuptsikevich adds.(joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0903 ET - The Canadian dollar falls against the U.S. dollar after data showed Canada's economy shrank by more than expected in the second quarter. GDP fell at an annualized rate of 1.6% in the April-June period following downwardly revised growth of 2.0% in the previous quarter. Economists in a WSJ survey expected a contraction of 0.7%. The figures were published at the same time as U.S. data that showed the U.S. core personal consumption expenditures price index rose 2.9% year-on-year in July, as expected, following a 2.8% rise in June. That's the Federal Reserve's preferred measure of inflation. The U.S. dollar rises 0.2% to an intraday high of 1.3779 Canadian dollars from 1.3754 before the data. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0901 ET - Issuance of new euro covered bonds--which include certain assets as collateral in case the issuer defaults--is expected to increase to 30 billion euro ($35 billion) in the final four months of 2025, ING's Maureen Schuller says in a note. Supply during the same period last year was lower due to the U.S. elections and tight covered bond spreads versus supranationals, Schuller says. "With 30 billion euro further supply, the full year 2025 will end at a 148 billion euro print for euro benchmark covered bonds." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0901 ET - Dell posted a significant 3Q revenue beat on the back of demand for its AI servers, but missed expectations for its gross margins and only slightly increased operating income guidance. That could fuel concerns that Dell is aggressively trying to gain share of the demand for AI servers at the expense of margins, say J.P. Morgan analysts. AI server customers are concentrated, potentially forcing providers to pursue "large AI server deals at aggressive margins and increasing risk to execution and margins in trying to deliver to the high and lumpy demand on a quarterly basis from customers," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0855 ET - Investors are grappling with Marvell Technology's second consecutive quarter of disappointing results and guidance, Benchmark analyst Cody Acree says in a research note. In the near-term, the semiconductor company will likely continue to struggle with destocking and increasing competition, according to Acree, who doesn't see a growth catalyst arriving until at least the next earnings report. Despite these upcoming challenges, though, he says Marvell's long-term outlook remains strong. The company is likely in a multi-year growth phase, "driven by the significant diversification of its design-win and project engagement pipeline across a growing list of sizable customers," Acree writes. Shares tumble 14% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0826 ET - President Trump's secondary tariffs on India have had minimal impact on global oil market fundamentals and are unlikely to significantly affect prices going forward, economists at Capital Economics say. "Seaborne shipments from Russia to India have fallen somewhat in the last few weeks, but exports to China have picked up a little this month and total Russian seaborne exports remain well within the range of the last 18 months," says Lily Millard. Still, the risk is that similar tariffs could be imposed to China--the largest importer of Russian crude--a move that could significantly disrupt global flows, according to the firm. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0817 ET - Gap logged solid 2Q results, but the report left investors feeling uneasy, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. On one hand, the apparel company's namesake brand has strong momentum thanks to a recent social media campaign, and its Old Navy brand is quickly growing. But that acceleration may be hard to maintain, the analysts say. At the same time, the company says tariff headwinds are expected to continue, and that its Athleta brand's recovery is weighing on results. While there are some green shoots, the analysts say they can't be overly optimistic about the stock, as performance across certain brands remains a mixed bag and visibility into profit growth is limited. Jefferies lowers its price target to $22 from $26. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0812 ET - European quality stocks are likely to rally as global growth starts to slow down, Bank of America equity strategists say in a note. The stocks have underperformed the market since 2021 as strong economic growth drove risk-on sentiment. As growth concerns resurface, investors are likely to favor quality European equities, boosting their performance, BofA says. "We see upside potential for pharma, luxury and [semiconductors], the three key European quality sectors." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0806 ET - Strong earnings from larger retailers and positive comments from their executives in recent weeks show that the consumer is still feeling good, say Mizuho analysts David Bellinger and Declan Kelley. "From our vantage point, consumer spending remained on a solid pace in Q2 and has continued throughout the month of August," the analysts say. The analysts note positive commentary about consumer sentiment from retailers Walmart, Home Depot, Lowe's, Dick's Sporting Goods, Best Buy, Dollar General and Five Below. The analysts say that while risks remain in the second half as tariff costs increase, "the consumer is alive, well, and still spending." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Canada 10-Year Bond Yield Drops to 2-Week Low
data.description:
The yield on the Canadian 10 year government bond has dropped toward 3.4%, marking a two-week low as markets revalued Canada’s growth and policy trajectory downwards even as US term premia and nominal rates pushed higher.

Statistics Canada’s Q2 report showing a negative 0.4% quarter on quarter GDP print with exports plunging about 7.5% and weaker business investment has materially lowered the path for Canadian policy and lifted the probability of BoC easing.

In contrast US Treasury yields rose on an updated US growth impulse and higher term premia stemming from stronger than expected Q2 GDP and the political headlines that have pushed some investors to demand more compensation at the long end, which widened the Canada US spread.

Though timing on its rebound is uncertain, the decision to focus on strategic growth in China and the U.S. is a bright spot, they add. Shares fall 1.5% to 53.85 euros. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0919 ET - Now that Remy Cointreau faces 15% tariffs from the U.S., instead of 30%, the French liquor company's decision to reinvest the difference is a key positive, Jefferies analysts write in a note. Remy reduced its estimates of the U.S. tariff hit on fiscal 2026 operating profit to 20 million euros, down from 35 million euros. As the cognac industry recovers in the medium term, the maker of Louis XIII cognac will too, analysts say. Though timing on its rebound is uncertain, the decision to focus on strategic growth in China and the U.S. is a bright spot, they add. Shares fall 1.5% to 53.85 euros. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0904 ET - Gold futures trade sideways, and are on track to end the week higher on interest rate cut expectations and safe-haven demand. Futures are flat on $3,475.30 a troy ounce, and are up 1.7% on week. The precious metal is trading at the upper end of its recent range of price. This reflects Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's signals about a U.S. rate cut, unprecedented political pressure from the White House on the Fed and persistently high levels of geopolitical tension, FxPro's Alex Kuptsikevich says in a note. President Trump's introduction of 50% tariffs against India risks deepening international divisions, as well as encourages the process of de-dollarization and diversification of central banks' foreign holdings, Kuptsikevich adds.(joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0826 ET - President Trump's secondary tariffs on India have had minimal impact on global oil market fundamentals and are unlikely to significantly affect prices going forward, economists at Capital Economics say. "Seaborne shipments from Russia to India have fallen somewhat in the last few weeks, but exports to China have picked up a little this month and total Russian seaborne exports remain well within the range of the last 18 months," says Lily Millard. Still, the risk is that similar tariffs could be imposed to China--the largest importer of Russian crude--a move that could significantly disrupt global flows, according to the firm. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0817 ET - Gap logged solid 2Q results, but the report left investors feeling uneasy, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. On one hand, the apparel company's namesake brand has strong momentum thanks to a recent social media campaign, and its Old Navy brand is quickly growing. But that acceleration may be hard to maintain, the analysts say. At the same time, the company says tariff headwinds are expected to continue, and that its Athleta brand's recovery is weighing on results. While there are some green shoots, the analysts say they can't be overly optimistic about the stock, as performance across certain brands remains a mixed bag and visibility into profit growth is limited. Jefferies lowers its price target to $22 from $26. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0708 ET - Gold futures fall, but remain close to all-time highs on escalating concerns about the U.S. Federal Reserve's independence. Futures are down 0.3% at $3,464.0 a troy ounce, but are on track to end the week 1.3% higher. The precious metal trades at the upper end of a range in place since mid-May, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. Following President Trump's move to dismiss Fed Governor Lisa Cook, the market is becoming increasingly concerned the Fed's independence is being undermined, analysts say. This is driving a flight to safe-haven assets like gold. Still, gold is unlikely to sustain its breach of the recent trading range just yet, Commerzbank says. Recent political and economic turmoil is relatively unlikely to be repeated and interest-rate cut expectations are already mostly priced in, analysts say. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0457 ET - Oil's outlook remains bearish, with WTI net longs standing at their lowest level since the 2008 financial crisis amid concerns over the impact of U.S. tariffs and a looming supply surplus, according to BMI. "U.S. Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell's dovish Jackson Hole speech helped to boost risk assets, and expectations for an upcoming U.S. rate cut and a weaker dollar may have lent some support to Brent," analysts at the firm say. "However, investors are unlikely to back any sustained rally in oil against a looming supply glut." BMI forecasts Brent to average $68 a barrel this year and $67 a barrel the next, but says risks are tilted to the downside. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

2312 ET - The Singapore dollar is steady against its U.S. counterpart in the morning session. Investors turn their attention to Friday's data on U.S. July PCE data, the Fed's preferred measure of inflation and the next key data ahead of the central bank's September policy meeting. The data could offer more clues on the pace of Fed rate cuts this year, Commerzbank Research analysts say in a note. USD/SGD is little changed at 1.2825, LSEG data shows. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

1905 ET - Fed governor Christopher Waller, the first Fed governor to speak publicly since President Trump moved to fire Lisa Cook on Monday, declines to comment on her situation. "Things are now in the hands of lawyers and the courts," Waller says, answering a question about the situation from a moderator after a speech delivered in Miami on Thursday evening. Earlier Thursday, Cook filed a lawsuit in federal court to keep her job. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1754 ET - Caterpillar raises its expected cost for tariffs this year after hundreds of imported finished goods containing steel and aluminum were made eligible for 50% U.S. duties on the metal in them earlier this month. The construction equipment maker now expects to pay $1.5 billion to $1.8 billion in 2025, up from $1.3 billion to $1.5 billion expected earlier. The company says its 3Q tariffs expenses will be about $500 million to $600 million. The revised tariff outlook is not expected to affect the Caterpillar's sales forecast. Shares down 3.6% in after-hours trading. (robert.tita@wsj.com)

1625 ET - The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board postpones implementation of tighter rules on auditors' quality controls following pushback from audit firms. The rule will take effect on Dec. 15, 2026 instead of Dec. 15 this year, the audit watchdog says. The PCAOB adopted the rule in May 2024, followed by SEC approval. Firms will have to assess the risks associated with achieving effective quality controls and design procedures intended to address those risks. The Center of Audit Quality, which represents firms that audit public companies, asked to delay the implementation to allow firms more time to prepare. The SEC, which oversees the PCAOB, is seeking candidates for all five board seats, including the chair. Erica Williams resigned as PCAOB chair last month at SEC Chair Paul Atkins' request.(mark.maurer@wsj.com; @markgmaurer)

1211 ET - Williams-Sonoma may have to raise prices to offset rising tariffs, but it will likely do so selectively, identifying products whose demand is least sensitive to higher prices, say UBS analysts. For new or highly unique products, customers will have a higher willingness to pay up and also have fewer comparisons for what the previous prices of these products were. "It's likely harder to take prices up on items that are more commoditized, such as a plain bath towel," the analysts say. Williams-Sonoma's customers are also likely less sensitive to price changes with its average customer household income around $140,000. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1157 ET - Williams-Sonoma maintained its operating margin guidance for the year despite its tariff rate doubling from May, which is impressive, say UBS analysts. But even before factoring additional furniture tariff rates that President Trump has threatened, tariff impacts should grow as the year progresses. The company's accounting method delays the impact of higher product costs, while it pulled forward some of its inventory in the first half of the year, subjecting it to lower tariffs. Assuming Williams-Sonoma has to absorb 15% to 30% of tariff costs, it could see a 135 to 260 basis point hit to margins, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)
